---
title: "Children's Health Crisis: Access to Care in a World Left Behind"
subtitle: "A UNICEF Data Story on Acute Respiratory Infections Among Under-5 Children"
author: "Data Analyst, UNICEF"
date: today
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    code-fold: true
    theme: flatly
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Contents"
    number-sections: false
    fig-width: 10
    fig-height: 6
    highlight-style: github
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
---

## Introduction

Every breath matters — especially for the **world's youngest children**.

Acute Respiratory Infections (**ARI**) are one of the leading causes of child mortality globally, responsible for millions of preventable deaths each year among children **under the age of five**. Yet despite this enormous burden, millions of families in low-income countries never reach a health facility or provider when their child is sick.

This report uses **UNICEF data** spanning over two decades and more than 125 countries to explore a simple but urgent question:

> *When a child under 5 shows symptoms of ARI — are caregivers able to seek help?*

The answer reveals deep and troubling **global inequalities** — rooted in poverty, geography, and the fragility of health systems. Countries like **Argentina and Cuba** see over 92% of families successfully seeking care. Meanwhile, in **Chad, Somalia, and Ethiopia**, fewer than 1 in 4 children receive any attention from a health provider.

This is not just a health story. It is a story about **access, equity, and the urgent need for action**.

---

## Data & Methodology

Three datasets from UNICEF were used in this analysis:

- **`unicef_indicator_1.csv`** — Percentage of children under 5 with ARI symptoms for whom care was sought from a health facility or provider, broken down by country, year, and sex.
- **`unicef_indicator_2.csv`** — Estimated rate of annual AIDS-related deaths per 100,000 population (ages 10–19).
- **`unicef_metadata.csv`** — Country-level socioeconomic indicators including GDP per capita, population, life expectancy, and birth rate.

All data transformation was performed using **Polars**, a high-performance DataFrame library for Python. Visualisations were built with **plotnine**, a Python implementation of the Grammar of Graphics.

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| echo: true

import polars as pl
import polars.selectors as cs
from plotnine import *
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

# ── Load data ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ind1 = pl.read_csv("unicef_indicator_1.csv")
ind2 = pl.read_csv("unicef_indicator_2.csv")
meta = pl.read_csv("unicef_metadata.csv")

# ── Basic data quality check ───────────────────────────────────────────────
print(f"Indicator 1  : {ind1.shape[0]:>5} rows × {ind1.shape[1]} cols")
print(f"Indicator 2  : {ind2.shape[0]:>5} rows × {ind2.shape[1]} cols")
print(f"Metadata     : {meta.shape[0]:>5} rows × {meta.shape[1]} cols")
print(f"\nIndicator 1 — unique countries : {ind1['country'].n_unique()}")
print(f"Indicator 1 — year range       : {ind1['time_period'].min()} – {ind1['time_period'].max()}")
print(f"Indicator 1 — sex categories   : {ind1['sex'].unique().to_list()}")
print(f"Indicator 1 — null obs_value   : {ind1['obs_value'].null_count()}")
```

---

## Global Picture: Who Gets Care?

The map below shows the **average ARI care-seeking rate** (%) for each country across all available years. Darker green indicates higher access to care; darker red flags the countries where children are most at risk of receiving no treatment at all.

The pattern is stark: **Sub-Saharan Africa and parts of South Asia** consistently show the lowest rates, while Latin America, Eastern Europe, and East Asia perform significantly better.

```{python}
#| label: fig-world-map
#| fig-cap: "Average ARI care-seeking rate (%) by country. Countries shown in grey had no data available."
#| echo: true

# Filter to 'Total' sex only, compute per-country average
map_data = (
    ind1
    .filter(pl.col("sex") == "Total")
    .group_by(["country", "alpha_3_code"])
    .agg(pl.col("obs_value").mean().round(1).alias("avg_ari"))
)

# plotnine world map requires map_data from plotnine itself
from plotnine import *
from plotnine.data import world  # built-in world map data

# Merge on ISO-3 code equivalent — plotnine 'world' uses 'name', join on alpha_3_code via country
# We'll use the 'map_id' approach (country names in plotnine world data)
world_df = world.copy()

# Merge: plotnine world uses 'name' column
map_merged = world_df.merge(
    map_data.to_pandas(),
    left_on="region",
    right_on="country",
    how="left"
)

(
    ggplot(map_merged, aes(x="long", y="lat", group="group", fill="avg_ari"))
    + geom_polygon(color="white", size=0.15)
    + scale_fill_gradient2(
        low="#d73027", mid="#fee08b", high="#1a9850",
        midpoint=60,
        na_value="#cccccc",
        name="Care rate (%)",
        limits=[0, 100]
    )
    + coord_fixed(1.3)
    + labs(
        title="ARI Care-Seeking Rate Among Under-5 Children",
        subtitle="Average across all available years | Grey = no data",
        x="", y=""
    )
    + theme_void()
    + theme(
        plot_title=element_text(size=14, face="bold"),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=10, color="#555555"),
        legend_position="bottom",
        legend_title=element_text(size=9),
        figure_size=(11, 5.5)
    )
)
```

---

## The Best and the Worst

The gap between the highest and lowest performers is **staggering**. The bar chart below ranks the **top 15 and bottom 15 countries** by average care-seeking rate.

Countries like **Argentina (94.3%), Macedonia (93.0%), and Cuba (92.9%)** are near-universal in their reach. At the other end, **Botswana (14.0%), Somalia (17.8%), and Chad (20.7%)** represent a public health emergency — where the majority of sick children never see a health professional.

```{python}
#| label: fig-bar-chart
#| fig-cap: "Top 15 and bottom 15 countries ranked by average ARI care-seeking rate."
#| echo: true

# Country averages (Total sex only)
country_avg = (
    ind1
    .filter(pl.col("sex") == "Total")
    .group_by(["country", "alpha_3_code"])
    .agg(pl.col("obs_value").mean().round(1).alias("avg_ari"))
    .sort("avg_ari", descending=True)
)

top15    = country_avg.head(15).with_columns(pl.lit("Top 15").alias("group"))
bottom15 = country_avg.tail(15).with_columns(pl.lit("Bottom 15").alias("group"))
bar_df   = pl.concat([top15, bottom15]).to_pandas()

# Order countries by avg_ari for the plot
bar_df["country"] = bar_df.apply(
    lambda r: r["country"][:20] + "…" if len(r["country"]) > 20 else r["country"], axis=1
)
bar_df = bar_df.sort_values("avg_ari")

(
    ggplot(bar_df, aes(x="reorder(country, avg_ari)", y="avg_ari", fill="group"))
    + geom_col(width=0.75)
    + geom_text(aes(label="avg_ari"), ha="left", size=7.5, nudge_y=0.8)
    + scale_fill_manual(values={"Top 15": "#1a9850", "Bottom 15": "#d73027"}, name="")
    + coord_flip()
    + labs(
        title="Who Gets Care? Top & Bottom 15 Countries",
        subtitle="Average ARI care-seeking rate (%) — children under 5",
        x="", y="Average care-seeking rate (%)"
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        plot_title=element_text(size=13, face="bold"),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=10, color="#555555"),
        legend_position="top",
        axis_text_y=element_text(size=8),
        figure_size=(10, 9)
    )
)
```

---

## Wealth and Health: Is There a Link?

It would be reasonable to assume that **wealthier countries** provide better healthcare access for children. The scatterplot below tests this assumption directly by plotting **GDP per capita** (constant 2015 USD) against **ARI care-seeking rates**, with a linear regression line to reveal the trend.

Each point represents a country–year observation. The regression line shows a **positive relationship**: as GDP per capita increases, care-seeking rates tend to rise. However, the wide spread of data points — especially in the low-to-middle income range — confirms that **wealth alone is not destiny**. Some low-income countries outperform their GDP peers, pointing to the critical role of **health system design, community health workers, and political will**.

```{python}
#| label: fig-scatter
#| fig-cap: "GDP per capita vs. ARI care-seeking rate. Each dot is a country-year observation. The blue line is a linear regression with 95% confidence interval."
#| echo: true

# Join ind1 (Total) with metadata on country + year
ind1_total = ind1.filter(pl.col("sex") == "Total")

scatter_df = (
    ind1_total
    .join(
        meta.select(["alpha_3_code", "year", "GDP per capita (constant 2015 US$)", "Life expectancy at birth, total (years)"]),
        left_on=["alpha_3_code", "time_period"],
        right_on=["alpha_3_code", "year"],
        how="inner"
    )
    .rename({
        "GDP per capita (constant 2015 US$)": "gdp_pc",
        "Life expectancy at birth, total (years)": "life_exp"
    })
    .filter(pl.col("gdp_pc").is_not_null())
    .to_pandas()
)

(
    ggplot(scatter_df, aes(x="gdp_pc", y="obs_value"))
    + geom_point(alpha=0.35, color="#2166ac", size=1.8)
    + geom_smooth(method="lm", color="#d73027", fill="#f4a582", size=1.2)
    + scale_x_log10(labels=lambda l: [f"${x:,.0f}" for x in l])
    + labs(
        title="Does Wealth Predict Healthcare Access?",
        subtitle="GDP per capita (log scale) vs. ARI care-seeking rate — each dot = one country-year",
        x="GDP per capita (log scale, constant 2015 USD)",
        y="ARI care-seeking rate (%)"
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        plot_title=element_text(size=13, face="bold"),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=10, color="#555555"),
        figure_size=(10, 6)
    )
)
```

---

## Progress Over Time: A Story of Two Trajectories

Has the situation improved over time? The time-series chart below tracks the **annual ARI care-seeking rate** for a selection of countries from different income brackets. This allows us to observe both **progress** and **stagnation** over two decades.

- **High-performing countries** like Argentina and Belarus show **consistently high and stable** rates throughout the 2000s–2020s.
- **Countries like Nigeria and Ethiopia** have seen **modest gains** but remain far below the 70% threshold.
- Notably, **Somalia** shows extreme year-to-year volatility — a reflection of ongoing conflict and collapsed health infrastructure.

The **global average** (dashed line) has moved upward over time, but the pace is too slow to meet children's needs.

```{python}
#| label: fig-timeseries
#| fig-cap: "ARI care-seeking rate (%) over time for selected countries, plus the global annual average (dashed line)."
#| echo: true

# Selected countries to highlight
selected = ["Nigeria", "Ethiopia", "India", "Argentina", "Bangladesh", "Somalia", "Bolivia"]

ts_selected = (
    ind1
    .filter((pl.col("sex") == "Total") & (pl.col("country").is_in(selected)))
    .select(["country", "time_period", "obs_value"])
    .to_pandas()
)

# Global annual average
global_avg = (
    ind1
    .filter(pl.col("sex") == "Total")
    .group_by("time_period")
    .agg(pl.col("obs_value").mean().round(1).alias("obs_value"))
    .with_columns(pl.lit("Global Average").alias("country"))
    .to_pandas()
)

import pandas as pd
ts_df = pd.concat([ts_selected, global_avg], ignore_index=True)

# Flag for line type
ts_df["line_type"] = ts_df["country"].apply(lambda c: "Global Average" if c == "Global Average" else "Country")

(
    ggplot(ts_df, aes(x="time_period", y="obs_value", color="country", linetype="line_type"))
    + geom_line(size=1.1)
    + geom_point(data=ts_df[ts_df["line_type"] == "Country"], size=2.2, alpha=0.8)
    + scale_linetype_manual(values={"Global Average": "dashed", "Country": "solid"}, guide=False)
    + scale_color_manual(
        values={
            "Nigeria":        "#e41a1c",
            "Ethiopia":       "#ff7f00",
            "India":          "#984ea3",
            "Argentina":      "#1a9850",
            "Bangladesh":     "#377eb8",
            "Somalia":        "#a65628",
            "Bolivia":        "#f781bf",
            "Global Average": "#333333"
        },
        name="Country"
    )
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=range(2000, 2025, 4))
    + labs(
        title="ARI Care-Seeking Rate Over Time",
        subtitle="Selected countries vs. global annual average (dashed) — children under 5",
        x="Year", y="Care-seeking rate (%)"
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        plot_title=element_text(size=13, face="bold"),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=10, color="#555555"),
        legend_position="right",
        figure_size=(11, 6)
    )
)
```

---

## Conclusion

The data tells a clear and troubling story.

**Access to basic healthcare for the world's sickest children is not evenly distributed** — it is deeply shaped by geography, national wealth, and the strength of local health systems. A child born in Chad or Somalia faces dramatically different odds of receiving care for a respiratory infection than a child born in Argentina or Ukraine.

Key takeaways from this analysis:

- The **global average care-seeking rate** has improved over the past two decades, but large disparities remain.
- There is a **positive correlation between GDP per capita and care-seeking rates**, yet outliers exist — proving that policy choices and health system investment can transcend economic constraints.
- **Sub-Saharan Africa** consistently lags behind other regions, with several countries stuck below the 30% mark for years.
- **Time-series trends** show that progress is possible — countries like India and Bangladesh have made meaningful gains — but the pace remains insufficient.

UNICEF's mandate is clear: **every child deserves care, regardless of where they are born**. Closing this gap requires targeted investment in community health workers, supply chains, and the removal of financial and geographic barriers that keep families from seeking help.

> *"The true measure of a nation's standing is how well it attends to its children."* — UNICEF

---

*Data source: UNICEF Data Warehouse. Analysis conducted in Python using Polars and plotnine.*
